---
title: "Fringed Star Grass (Hypoxis juncea)"
slug: "hypoxis-juncea"
group: "Plantae"
image: "https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/623787541/medium.jpg"        # keep for thumbnails
image-alt: "Fringed Star Grass"  # keep for a11y
tax_kingdom: "Plantae"
tax_class: "Liliopsida"
tax_order: "Asparagales"
tax_family: "Hypoxidaceae"
categories: ["Plantae", "Liliopsida", "Asparagales", "Hypoxidaceae"]   # these drive the chips + filtering
freeze: true
---

<figure class="species-figure">

<img src="https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/623787541/medium.jpg" alt="Fringed Star Grass" class="species-photo"/>

<figcaption class="photo-credit">

Photo: <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/people/garccyy">
garccyy</a> (CC BY-NC) via <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/342530486">iNaturalist</a>

</figcaption>

</figure>

## Taxonomy

-   **Class:** Liliopsida
-   **Order:** Asparagales
-   **Family:** Hypoxidaceae

## Occurrence at DeLuca Preserve

-   [DeLuca Bio Blitz Species Observations](https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?project_id=191727&subview=map&taxon_id=126632)

## Conservation & rarity context

-   IUCN: Not Listed
-   **Rarity note:** Common species in Florida found in dry to mesic sandy habitats.

## Natural history notes

A small, grass-like wildflower characterized by its narrow leaves and bright yellow, star-shaped flowers with fringed petal edges. In Florida, it is found in dry to mesic sandy habitats such as flatwoods, sandhills, and open pine woodlands. It typically blooms during the warmer months and occurs as a subtle component of groundcover vegetation in fire-maintained ecosystems.

## References / links

-   [iNaturalist species page](https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/126632-Hypoxis-juncea)
-   [Local profile](https://www.wildflower.org/plants/result.php?id_plant=HYJU)
